Dave, Dave D asked me if I had done my Tony Robbins energy yells and I told him Bill does the yells for both of us so you missed that he was out earlier in the hall doing his yells and his woe yeas or ye woes or you know hand claps and all that stuff so if you had gotten here earlier you could have seen that. I said last night to the IBAs I spent all day yesterday with a client, Harvey Zemmel who is here from London and all day long it occurred to me you know the proximity of doing a consulting day with somebody and then the next day teaching them what you were doing to them you know probably would have been better with a little time lapse you know gone by but so here we are and for starters a reminder of what you are at which you are not at is a session about any of the basics of either the consulting or the coaching business any that work their way in will be by accident or has part of an answer to a question but I will limit that so there is a certain amount of presumed knowledge in the room and if you lack it you will have to go get it after the fact backwards so like I am not going to spend much time on the 12 ways you can structure a coaching program and you know that sort of thing. This is largely about the handling of the client whether that and it does not matter whether we are talking about the client as a member of a coaching program or the client is a one on one coaching or consulting relationship I will use the word client interchangeably and most of what we talk about applies to any and all of those circumstances and so mostly we are going to be talking about the getting of although most of that will be tomorrow and the control of and the extracting of maximum money from and the keeping of and the multiplying of and so that is what we are going to spend most of our time on and some of it will be oh I don't know emotionally uncomfortable for some of you I am sure because I am not going to take the time to every time I talk about a tactic I am not going to take the time to make you feel ethically morally spiritually and metaphysically healthy about it I will take the time to do that maybe two or three times during the entire day but so some of what we are going to be talking about I mean is literally you know about how to without them noticing it pick their pockets and if that is really troubling to you you know you may have a tough day there is a certain amount of when I prepared this there is a certain amount of what I call the world's longest beard problem which is an old story about the guy with the world's longest beard and they go to interview him up in the mountains of West Virginia and the interviewer asks him does he sleep with the beard under the covers or outside the covers and he never got a good night's sleep again and so there is a certain amount of things that I long ago stopped doing consciously that I now do unconsciously you know I have trained myself to do them and do them all the time and never give them any thought and I realize I am actually now doing them but I don't need to do them anymore and actually it is not necessarily congruent with my objectives and I am still doing it because I ingrained it but it is sometimes hard to consciously back all that out and enunciate it in conscious instructions I have done my best but again you will get more out of me if you ask me questions because you may provoke something that I did not occur to me while I was preparing you may have observed something about what I do or what Bill does or what Glaser Kennedy does that we are doing unconsciously and you and I may not mention it unless you ask about it and by the way most people including most Glaser Kennedy members come and go viewing us on only one level so if they come to the super conference they like get out of it what is on the educational motivational entertaining level for them and so they hear the speakers they take notes about what the speakers say they meet people they network etc. what they don't do on a simultaneous mental track is observe how we are doing that super conference and try and figure out why we are doing this this way and why we are doing this this way I have one client for years who you know his whole note taking thing if he has like three pads and one is content one is why are they doing it the way they are doing it and one is how can I apply that to my business so the thing that you should know and that will become abundantly apparent from me during the two days that we are together is that pretty much nothing you see us do is just like accidental or random it is all pretty strategic and thought out and to the point of unconscious now it was originally consciously thought out but to use a conference as an example I mean there is meticulous thought about body movement and where this thing gets positioned in a room and what speaker goes in front of what speaker and how many minutes everybody gets and what gets said first when somebody steps to the stage and on and on and on and on and on and so the same is true pretty much with everything I do and so if you are really really astute and you really make a point of it there is a sort of multi-layered educational value in getting the newsletter for example I mean you can read it for pure entertainment and it is built to facilitate that you can read it just to get marketing ideas and examples but then you could read it on the third layer of how is it structured and why is it being done in a certain way in order to get a member keep a member ascend a member get money from a member and so really there is a whole nother way to study that piece that you get from us that most people it does not occur to them and quite frankly that is okay I am not sure it is beneficial to us to have it occur to everybody I am just pointing it out to you because you paid your money I mean I am not actually sure that having done this was very smart with 2020 hindsight as I prepared it but you know here we are so there is this layer of what are they up to why did they do this why did he talk about this why did he put this in the newsletter with etc that for the people in this room I think is sort of important and that you ought to be paying attention to you have a big fat you know take home book in front of you we are mostly going to work out of the book today we are going to deal with sections one through three and some of the sample documents and tomorrow we are going to deal with sections four and five and the rest of the sample documents so let's get started if you jump there are page numbers shortly the first few pages in front of section one are numbered so if you just jump into I don't know just a couple pages in two or three pages in you are going to find a page that looks like this and I just wanted to briefly mention some of the things that are very very relevant to what we are going to be talking about in the next two days one is that I have been in a consulting business like since the beginning I did something else for one year after getting out of high school and then at least some part of my life was consulting and taking money from people for dispensing advice and opinions and eventually dispensing work product in the earliest years and in the years when I paid the least attention to it there wasn't a year that I made less than 100k and that goes back to 1975 when 100k was some serious money and in terms of coaching we didn't call it that then but my earliest experience with coaching was in what was called and still is called practice management in chiropractic and dental and in what year? 1977, 1978, 1979, somewhere in there we were we were selling a $30,000 what we would now call coaching program to chiropractors when $30,000 was serious money and it's interesting that in a sense nothing much changes this is the old original brochure from that and here's what they got for their $30,000 they got actually we gave more than we do today they got they got eight mastermind meetings they got two staff training seminars they got system-oriented training meaning what you do in your office which really is a fancy word for they got a big notebook like you just got they got non-system training meaning the head trip stuff and communication skills and so forth which really means they got a second big notebook like you just got they got a complete set of audio and video materials they got a monthly newsletter they got a monthly staff newsletter they had monthly accountability which is pretty painless giving them because only like two percent of them use monthly accountability so monthly accountability in this coaching program was fill out a four-page form about what happened in your practice this month and fax it in before your one-on-one coaching call well you never saw forms because if you asked people to do something continuing telephone consulting so they had a they had a coach assigned to them that they talked to on a regular basis and an initial training like a fast start day that they came to and so we were selling that for $30,000 in either $77,000, $78,000 or $79,000 I lose track and at peak that business had 600 and some odd doctors at $30,000 a piece a year so this is that this is not like new and what may be new to you isn't anymore than the Tony Yell is new it goes back a long long long way at least to the 19 Emil Coe Dave what 30s 40s 30s so there's not a lot new under the sun it may be new to you it's certainly new to your clients it keeps getting kind of a new wrapper around it but a lot of what we're doing and a lot of what you're going to see during these two days has has has has some pretty deep roots significant for me and significant about what we're going to talk about is is that I maintain over 70 percent of my client relationships for long periods of years there are clients with me for 10 years there's clients with me for 20 years there's a few with me going on 25 years and even has my compensation demands have escalated and escalated and escalated far out of pace with the rate of inflation they stay and they keep coming back now I don't mention that so much to brag has to say to you there's like a science to that because the getting of clients is the most expensive thing you'll ever do and and initially if all of that is new to you it'll be an exciting thing to do when somebody says yes and somebody writes out a check and signs a contract or somebody joins a program you will be incredibly excited and enthused about that but give it a few years and it will be a routine and necessary event it will not be the thing that is exciting and so the retention of the client becomes ever more important and so I've got people in coaching who've been with me for 10 years in coaching groups and and what is is an aside what is significant about that is that they've long since stopped being there for the core reasons of the coaching they could all conduct a coaching meeting in my absence so their retention is about something other than that and it is the something other than that that is pretty important and the third part of that is is that all of that goes on with me being very dictatorial and and as one client pointed out you could make that a play on words in my in my relationships with my clients and so they're staying around and continuing to give me money and asking to give me money more than I will take it and asking me to do more than I am willing to do with me dictating to them virtually every aspect of the relationship and and being rather difficult to deal with by most standards now there's two lessons in that and we'll get to them as we go along I have so some people will say well you can do that with entrepreneurs but you can't do it with corporate clients you can I have had and have now major corporate clients I don't seek them I haven't had a lot of them because they're a giant pain in the rear but you can do it everything we'll talk about in these two days is equally applicable to a fortune 1 000 client if that happens to be the kind of field you're in has it is to an entrepreneur client or has it is if you're a high-end financial planner or you're a cosmetic dentist or you're a cosmetic surgeon to the individual consumer you might be sitting across the desk from all of the same principles all of the same strategies apply equally and so my clients have included fortune 5's fortune ones those giving a million at a time instead of 30 000 at a time the zeros the numbers change the strategies the arrangements the management of the client does not change and so it's very important to get the my business is different my client is different my customer is different out of your head if at all possible sometime in the next five minutes so it doesn't get in your way during the next 20 some odd hours and I got to tell you everybody in this room conceptually knows that that should be out of your way many of you are now turning around and teaching it to people that know your business is not different but that doesn't mean you still don't have in your head my business is different I did two consulting days this week and one of them I did with people who've been with me for a number of years and are pretty successful and pretty smart and conceptually know it intellectually know it and yet sit there the whole day saying things that reflect a belief system that yeah but our clients are really different and in fact I will hear off it for people I know our clients aren't our business isn't different but and then they will proceed to tell you the five reasons why their business is really different and so even those of you who are now turning around and teaching to other people that know their businesses really aren't different it's very easy to have it creep back into your own head about your business or your clients or your situation and anytime it creeps in it's self-sabotage it's in the way because what I'm talking about here is absolutely universal and and my career experience kind of proves it I would point out that these sort of methods work just as well for amateurs and novices as they do for experienced professionals and probably the best evidence that you can go from zero to sixty in coaching and or consulting without necessarily much background in it is is the Glacier Kennedy independent business advisor program that we created about half the room here is made up of people who are independent business advisors although they are all part-time some spare time they all have other things that they're doing some of them have other coaching or consulting businesses but some of them do not and they are lickety split got coaching groups formed and are making 10,000 a month 15,000 a month 20,000 a month in their local areas in some cases including people and I don't want to embarrass them but including people in the room who couldn't spell coach you know before we got them and and they're merrily coaching and taking money for it so you also should not have in your head that you know you needed other 13 years before you dare do some of these things almost everything that I'll talk about in these two days I'm mostly talking about what I do now but 90 percent of it is the same as what I did 10 years ago 20 years ago including when I could ill afford to do it economically or when I had absolutely no qualifications or recognition or any of that in order to base it on and so I'll often hear from people get to be Dan Kennedy I did that stuff it's not you gotta wait it really is this business is a lot about cart before horse not horse before cart the last thing I'll say sort of in preparation about almost nine months and then he went to 60 members in the two or three months so it can be done very quickly if you allow yourself to do that thank you gentlemen you may return okay who gets that's what I just did and more importantly why I did it yeah just this case hmm sure yeah exactly yeah I I took the time to whatever terminology you want to use for it but to establish authority even though you guys all know me and we already have a pre-existent relationship and obviously you pretty much bought into the fact that I have the right to teach this thing or you wouldn't have written the check and been here in the first place but in spite of all that I took the time to establish the specific authority for the specific topic that we're going to spend two days on in part through demonstration now why go to all that trouble right well first of all how long has it been since you read the sales letter that brought you here well for a few of you a day or two but for most of you perhaps probably weeks or a month or two months which means you don't remember the sales letter that brought you here so everything I said in the sales letter that got you here has long since left your mind and it isn't just about remaking the sale it is about getting control of you for the next today and so I have taken the time to go back through a re-establish my right to sit up here and talk to you about this topic right it's so it's an extremely important lesson and by lesson what I mean is is that it pretty much needs to happen every single time you cannot operate on the basis of gee we've had a relationship for about three years so they know me he knows me she knows me I don't need to resell them I don't need to remind them of why they're giving me money and I don't need to retake control yes you do virtually every single time so virtually every single time you put them in a coaching room every single time you sit down with them one on one there should be some exercise it may only be five minutes maybe all it takes but there should be some exercise of re-establishing what the relationship is meaning you whatever your position is brilliant genius experienced old grizzled wise veteran of your industry person extremely adept at making people rich in a hurry whatever your position is you need to re-establish it and you need to re-establish the relationship which is a me chief you Indian me instructor you student right and so and so don't don't miss the point let's start on page one which should be immediately after your your tabid divider you should find a page that looks like that that few very quick things to say about it just about everything that I do is completely contrary to what would be talked to you about the coaching or the consulting business from any traditional source so for example if you happen to go to coach university or some variation of it no disrespect to those folks or if you hang out in the coach subsection special interest group of the national speakers association or you go to the bookstore and buy a book about this you are going to find a a very different set of premises about these businesses than our premises first of all you are going to find that they are mired in a hours for dollars culture and so it's all about how to charge seventy five dollars an hour and talk to somebody on the phone it isn't this cool or how to make seven hundred fifty dollars a day or a thousand dollars a day it's so the the entire culture is all about trading a block of time for a block of money as a practical matter first of all that's not a business just so you know that's a job and secondly it has no leverage it has no multiplier effect therefore you will eventually reach a point where you actually can only get so much from them per hour or per dollar and regardless of value they won't give you anymore and you will eventually be unhappy with it because you will be capped and you there's always so many hours and you can only stand to do what you do so long and so ultimately every psychologist doesn't want to be a psychologist anymore and every lawyer doesn't want to be a lawyer anymore and every CPA doesn't want to be a CPA anymore because they're like stuck they get to a certain point and they're stuck and in this pure exchange of dollars for time from the clock from from your standpoint or from my standpoint see what we do for them we can't take enough money from them for it to be an accurate representation of the value so if if in a day I take a guy with a million-dollar a year business and I re-engineer the business so he goes home and has a 10 million-dollar a year business arguably I should have got nine million bucks for the day and he can start making his money the second year but see I don't care how good I am at everything I'm going to show you these two days getting somebody to write the nine million dollar check for the day it's like too much right I mean maybe there's somebody out there getting it that I don't know yet but it's like too much if you go to a therapist for six sessions and they save your marriage how much is that worth well it's worth a lot more than anybody is willing to pay now some therapists get a lot more than others because of these techniques but still the therapist is grossly underpaid the client ultimately feels they are overpaying because they are viewing it as an exchange of minutes for money and so Billy Bob who's your client sits down and figures out how much he's making in his business per hour right well I make 140 dollars an hour and I'm paying this lunatic five thousand dollars an hour something's wrong here right so the entire premise is like suicidal it eventually leads to everybody being unhappy the clients are unhappy you're unhappy everybody's unhappy so you got to get out of that box it has many different ways as you can and you should know that all of the traditional training and so if you go hang around with any of the traditionalists they are all about putting in that box and staying in that box and they have a oh I don't know a a a disapproving viewpoint of everything that is done outside of that box our 30 000 coaching program for doctors in the late 1970s who was sold his cash out of percentage above base so very quickly let me explain that to you percentage above base is a way of getting paid we'll talk about it later it's pros and it's cons and whatever but percentage above base is essentially in that case sitting down with the doctor and saying okay how much did you do last year okay I did a hundred I'll do simple numbers so I try and do I did a hundred thousand dollars okay and the rate of inflation is 10 so we're gonna say and and how much growth did you have last year over the year before 10 percent okay so rate of inflation 10 growth 10 so we're going to give you 20 so we're going to set your base at 120 000 for the first year of their agreement we're going to roll another 20 on it and set the base for 140 000 the second year we're going to put another 20 percent on it then and set it at 100 and whatever that would be 1468 178 whatever that is for the third year that's your base and now we get 30 percent of everything that gets created above base okay well they'll agree to that by the way because they're right and they don't believe it's going to happen anyway but then if you can convince them that they're going to go to a half a million every year and you pencil out what they're going to wind up paying you but they can discount all that out by giving you 30 000 all in one time it becomes pretty easy to get the 30 000 especially if you can manage to get in finance now you got to have somebody who sleeps on the couch with them and carries into the bank in the morning in order to get in finance but so that's that's how that's sold what's important about all that is I'm just giving you an example of how we got out of the box of talking about hours see what we're going to deliver now is irrelevant it's the money outcome that is relevant the second thing is is is that all of the traditionalists have an academic orientation about these businesses and by that I mean two things number one they're hung up on credentials and qualifications and certifications and and and and do you have the appropriate letters of the alphabet after your name to allow you to to consult with people in this subject area or to coach people in this subject area what is extremely important to understand about that is nobody cares except the people issuing the letters of the alphabet after the name no one else cares the marketplace does not care at all it's not an advantage it's not a disadvantage it's not and by and large they don't ask I'm maybe five times in 30 years have been directly asked if I went to college maybe five times and maybe another five times indirectly because they assume and so the question is what school did you go to you know oh where'd you graduate from I mean you know so the assumption is there but nobody has like refused to write the check so it doesn't matter but they're all about okay these the these permissions to go do these businesses and you've got to get you don't need anybody's permission to do any of it you just go do it the other thing is is that the academic orientation leads them to ladder thinking now ladder thinking is very important for you to get everybody else to do okay you want to show everybody you take money from ladders because they've all been conditioned to climb one if you stick it in front of them all right so you want to be sticking one in front of them and as soon as they get to the top of that one you want to be sticking another one in front of them and another one in front of them and another one in front of them because they'll climb it okay but it's very important for you never to climb on so you got to show other people ladders but if somebody shows you a ladder you got to laugh at it because you got to know that a bunch of us sat in the room and figured out the ladder and we figured out the ladder for one reason and one reason only to control people that's why we figured it out and so Boy Scouts of America has a ladder a bunch of people sat in a room and figured it out so they could control the kids and the parents that's what it's there for and it isn't there for any other reason and so it is with every single ladder including some I could mention that would probably offend some people so you know it's really really important for you to laugh at them but to make sure you construct them because it's one of the best ways to control people last they're all mired in poverty consciousness if you have most coach you folks if you had some coach you folks here and you had some NSA culture coaches here and you had some what's the other life coach institute graduates here and you had a few MBAs and Booz Allen consultants and you know we had 40 or 50 of them hanging around here and we let them hear just what was said at the table a few minutes ago they would either all run screaming from the room or they would be trying to get our certifications yanked from wherever the devil we got him in the first place right because how dare you charge that kind of money and you know I mean it would start there okay so they are mired in their own version of a poverty outlook they have in their head you should only be able to make so much an hour and that's so much better than what everybody else makes an hour so that's fair there's a sort of a you know there's a continuous conversation going on about the ratio between CEO pay and worker pay and how there should be some sort of formulaic multiple between what the CEO gets and should only be 16 times what the lowest paid worker gets it shouldn't be 18 times and it certainly shouldn't be 180 times and it certainly shouldn't be 1800 times what the lowest worker gets and this conversation by the way has been going on since the industrial revolution it ain't a new debate okay and and and it was being screamed about about Henry Ford and Andrew Carnegie then and it's being screamed about you know now admittedly the multiples gotten a little bigger but so has the value differential it ignores where does the value come from in the conversation well these people have sort of the same kind of multiple formula in their heads of okay our clients make x so we should only make oh a psychiatrist makes x so we should only make x a lawyer makes x so we should only make x and so if you hang around them that's what you get in your head and you don't want it there okay your monetary compensation is based on a combination of actual value his value is defined by the recipient not his it is defined by you and what you can get that those are the two criteria and so carothers is such a great example because $1,100 bucks $35,000 well a which would you rather have is it unconscionable well not if a sufficient number of people are willing to pay it that determines it's perfectly conscionable they should get to choose not the national board of college planning consultants if there is such a thing they shouldn't get a vote Lou Dobbs shouldn't get a vote if enough people are willing to pay $35,000 for that service then that tells you the service is worth $35,000 to enough people to at least support him and it immediately says gee maybe they'll pay $37,8 and that doesn't sound like much but how many deals did you do last year excuse me how many clients did you take care of last year well so if it's a hundred a year and we go from $35,000 to $37,000 it doesn't make any difference to the client but it does make $280,000 difference to him so you know you know we got to think about that right so you want to ignore almost everything you hear from traditional sources your next page in a sense almost everything we do today will have to do with this in another sense it won't because I'll be as practical and pragmatic as I can but the truth is most of the success in the coaching and consulting businesses has to do what's what's going on in your head more than anything else it has to do with your attitudes about yourself and about money your bravado the size of your cojones your ability to keep a straight face when you quote a fee those sorts of things and they really are self-image related they're attitudinal related and they are not really related to anything else so what's kind of important to understand is that there are no moorings for this business now in almost all other businesses they're mistaken too but a lot of businesses have you know sort of a relationship somehow that leads to price so in retail it's double it buy it for 50 cents sell it for 100 and direct response tv it's eight to 10 times and so the first thing that happens in a conversation about doing anything on direct response tv is we got to have an eight to ten times multiple of product cost so if it costs a buck we got to be able to sell it for eight dollars or ten dollars if we can't sell it for eight dollars or ten dollars we're not going to do it and we're probably not going to sell it for more and twelve dollars so there's like there's a relationship that has something to do with to something that has something to do with price right in this business there is no there are no such moorings there is no such relationship and to a great degree in the information product business there is no such mooring either just to prove that we could do it many many years ago i had a client who was a consultant uh on theft control to the supermarket and convenience store industry and we sold from the front of the room a single audio cassette tape and a number 10 envelope with a piece of paper in it for 500 dollars and they saw that's what they were getting i mean we didn't hide it so you held up a tape and you held up an envelope with a one piece of paper in it and you sold it for 500 dollars um uh now understand that you could you could sit with the folks at night yell conan for the next decade and not convince them that you could sell a single cassette tape and a piece of paper in an envelope for 500 dollars right you could sit at simon and chuster for a decade and you couldn't convince them that you could do this if you sat at nsa you might convince a few people that you could do it but then they would all report you to the ethics committee because they would think there was something unethical about selling a single cassette tape and a piece of paper and an envelope for 500 dollars um but see it has to do with what's on the cassette tape and what's on the piece of paper right it doesn't have to do with the media but his traditionally and historically there's a mooring there's okay how many tapes in the box times 10 times so much for the manual here's how we get to a price well this business has none of those moorings and so you got to forget that they're even there it all has to do with what's going on in here and when i say that that in order to succeed as a consultant coach you have to be one there's a certain this is about now the attitudes reflected in behavior there's a certain behavior that succeeds and a certain behavior that doesn't there's like an issue of presence all right um i mean everyone up here at the table it's by they would be dressed differently in most cases if they were like taking money from people at the moment although by the way well i'll talk about it when i get to costumes but there's a presence to them when they stand up and talk about what they do so this whole thing of of being the thing in order to get the money is pretty important and so a lot of what we're going to talk about today is about what's going on internally not just externally so let's talk about some attitudes that are absolutely necessary here's page three um first of all what is your role well your role as a consultant coach is not to be of service to clients being of service to clients implies you are a servant to clients that's that's not what we do our role is actually to control clients and to attempt to get compliance from clients i often tell the story of my experience as a patient with chiropractors and so the very first chiropractor i went to um i was having pretty severe back trouble at the time i go in and if you've ever how many of you've been to chiro okay you guys you you know all know the drill right so they bring in they show you the x-rays and they do the report of findings and they tell you you got to come in four times a week for the first three weeks and three times a week for the next six weeks and etc etc etc and i know the drill too i knew it when i went in so i had sort of mentally prepared because so the doc gets done with the whole presentation that's all fine except you know that's fine for ordinary people but that's no good for me because you know i got a bunch of stuff going on and sometimes i got to jump on an airplane with a mom's notice and i can't be scheduled on appointments a week ahead of time and you know it's all a bit of what we all get done we've arrived in an arrangement that i'll come in when i want to and i'll call them up a couple hours and they'll squeeze me in and uh how long you think i kept going uh not very damn long a few months later i now go to my second chiropractor because of course my back problem is worse and she does pretty much the same presentation i do pretty much the same presentation she now concludes the the presentation by excuse me walking out coming back in with the yellow pages opening it to chiropractors and say and have red pen in her hand and saying let me mark three or four doctors i'd be happy to refer you to who will accept you on that basis and are very good doctors here in the community but but you can't be a patient here at this practice on that basis huh right well because we you know a relationship period you you know we we're in a partnership to get you well i can't do my part if you won't do your part and i don't want any patients here who aren't going to get well so i'm not going to accept you as a patient well wait a minute maybe i can't arrange my schedule right uh when i went back for my first adjustment all chiropractors give you some kind of little xerox piece of paper with little drawings of exercises on it that you're supposed to do and they mark the number of reps on the blank line right you're supposed to do eight of these four of these which immediately goes under the car seat is where that goes um and so i go back for my first adjustment and dr penne says uh how you doing with those three exercises all great to fabulous you know well i come good at doing these exercises right get out of here you know uh well show me exercise number three i want to make sure you're doing it right i mean you got to fess up right but i ain't doing it i don't even know what exercise three is the sheets are to the car seat and i haven't got any intention to do it just crack my back crack my back and you are my neck and let me get out of here you know i got things to do now you got to go home you can't be a patient here if you're not going to do your exercises so we'll reschedule you for thursday and come back in and be prepared to show me all three exercises before you get an adjustment well you know i'm stopping and pissed off but i go do the exercises you know come back do the exercise so seeing most doctors would think that their role is to be of service to the patient but now the role is to be the superior being in control in order to get compliance from the patient so if you have an in-service attitude the relationship is ordered in the long term if you have in-service attitude you give up control to decline second attitude superiority nothing is more important to the successful consulting coach than complete disdain for everybody you're dealing with that doesn't mean you don't like it but you've got to really have in your head how incredibly ignorant and dysfunctional they all are and i used to somebody asked this question how much money do you have to take from somebody to get a smart person who can follow instructions and i said i used to think it was about that you know okay we're only taking 30,000 and they're behavioral ideas maybe if we took 300,000 they think they think they take it more seriously and they need to be better no it doesn't matter how much money do you take from them first of all the entire universe continually reorders itself into 80, 20 and 515 within the 20 it all of you reorders itself and what do you do there may be so much money you take from them doesn't make any difference how much time you spend with them doesn't make any difference how much money you deliver to them doesn't matter how much legitimate expertise you have but none of that matters every little group reorders itself to 515 and 80 and they keep doing it over and over and over again so you could put 10 billionaires in a coaching program and within three months you would realize that you had a sharp as a whip champion who was implementing everything and you had really slow students and they'd all be billionaires wouldn't make any difference right and every time i keep thinking well maybe if we take more money from them no they can't get out of their own way and actually by the way the ones running the big companies who are making 20 million dollars a year CEOs they're like far more dysfunctional than you and i i mean it's a miracle they believe me it's a good thing they got a limo picking them up in the morning and getting them to work there's a reason why those companies pay for that it ain't a perk it's like essential otherwise they'd be roaming around out in the street unable to find their way to the office with their wall street journal under their eye i mean so you look you got like the the first big like breakthrough and actually i'll tell you three quick stories one is in 1978 after a year of making money as a speaker doing things very dumb and very primitive and very painful i went to my first national speaker association workshop all excited because now i was finally going to go figure out you know how to do this smart and by and this is a three day workshop by the end of the first day i'm like totally depressed and spent the night in my hotel room instead of going anything because it quickly became apparent to me that just about everybody was a better speaker than i was but nobody knew how to make any money and in fact i was already making more money than most of them and i'd come to find out how to make more money and make it smarter and by the end of the day i was convinced they're all making less money than i am and they're doing even dumber shit than i'm doing so this is not good you know and so i'm like horribly depressed by the next day i'm like excited now i'm walking around thinking okay if these clowns doing what they're doing are like getting booked at all right by the third day i've convinced myself i'm an expert and i'm at the airport ready to go home and um uh uh uh you may he's deceased now buster crab he was flash gordon and tarzan way way way back went and buster crab had been at the meeting and he's sitting in the airport and we're both flying back to phoenix and uh he said so i say what did you think of the meeting and he says he says i'm just i'm i'm really depressed i said what are you depressed about he says well he says i've been doing this thing i've been getting some some bookings and i've been speaking and i got a little book but you know i know i'm not making what i should be making doing this and i came here to figure out i was going to be around all the smart pencils in the box and actually find out how to do what i'm doing smart and it turns out i'm i think i'm making more money than most of them and i don't think they're even doing half the things i'm doing smart you know what did you think of the meeting i said well i thought the same thing at a meeting he said well what's your background so i have an appetite so he says well how about if i hire you to redo all my stuff because you seem smarter than everybody else i'm in here so now i'm on the airplane i got a client right so i got my first consulting my first speaker is a consulting client because both of us came and decided everybody was a moron except us so so and he wants to give money to somebody and i'm like the least moron he met that's pretty much the deal okay well so now by the time i get home i'm okay well if he feels that way so now i start a newsletter you know and before you know it i got 300 speakers paying me uh 200 bucks a year for a newsletter and i'm off to the races right and and and my disdain for the population has never changed now that again many of them are far better speakers than i and many of them are far better at one thing or another than i am but generally speaking they're not any business and those of you who do business consulting generally speaking everybody in every population don't have any business sense my second experience i'm still in advertising business and i go to meet on a saturday with the ceo of the number one ad agency in cleveland which admittedly i know world's tallest midget but still it's it's it's you know it's never one ad agency in cleveland and i get there in this guy they own their own building and this guy is out sleeves rolled up it's a saturday so but he's cutting the grass in front of his building now first of all the guy's cutting his own grass right so unless this is your hobby which for some people it is but then you want to do it out of sight of anybody if you're the ceo of the largest ad agency so he's not cutting his own grass as i get there start talking to him the wheels goes bad on the lawnmower he picks the lawnmower up he drags it from the front of the office building with me tailing along talking to him all the way to the back of the office building where there's a little workshop where there's a ranch that he can tighten the wheel on a lawnmower and then drag it all the way back to the front of the building to cut the grass now this guy's the ceo of the largest ad agency in cleveland and he ain't got enough sense to go get the ranch right i'm immediately feeling smarter and more powerful in this conversation because i'm thinking this guy's an idiot wait watchers i get hired to work for wait watchers the president wait watchers at the time very nice fellow and i'm sure he was very good at something um i have no idea what but he was very good at something all right but we're all in a meeting where their ad agency bent and bartender stood in stupend who's taken a lot of money from him has come to show them their new tv commercials and they show these tv commercials these tv commercials are stick figure drawings of women talking to each other on a beach stick figures you can draw it with a crayon talking to each other on a beach and then a logo and the lights come up and everybody sits there waiting to see how he's going to react he says fabulous everybody says fabulous everybody goes on their own i said less that's the dumbest thing i've ever seen he said it is he said but it can't be it came from bent and bartender stood in stupend very powerful lesson there okay he don't know good advertising from bad advertising he hasn't got a clue all right but since they did it and since he gave him a lot of money to do it and they have a reputation in the advertising community it must be good all right this is like taking somebody to a really fancy restaurant the food's horrible but they think it's good because it has to be because they just paid 80 bucks for it and they're in a fancy place with six forks and a guy no bad food's bad food no matter who it came from right but but he don't know and i'm sitting there thinking this guy's running this whole company and he's an idiot you know and then would you pay for these things wow the production budget was a half a million dollars i mean you need a kid in the crayon you know you could have done this on an etch a sketch taking a picture of it i mean you could produce this thing for 10 grand that had money left over are you nuts huh that's what Benton Broughton Derson is stupid told me it cost real lessons there so you've got to realize that while your clients may be good at something and usually it's their technical competence or whatever it is that they do that in the common sense department which is mostly what coaching and consulting is all about you are far superior to all of them you are the superior being walking the planet as dysfunctional as you may be by comparison trust me you beat them all i mean i'm looking at the current crop of presidential candidates right is there any of them you would hire and put in charge of anything no not a one of them right and one of them is going to wind up running the country that country what's the number one qualification necessary in order to run the country the arrogance to think you should be running the country that's it i mean you got one guy who was a mayor you know who did a pretty good job as a mayor although he alienated every constituency you worked with and you know his personal life is a godawful mess and he's got all kinds of business and financial problems and pretty much what everybody's impressed about is that they got a mental picture in ahead of them with a hard hat on after 9 11 that's and we're going to put him in charge we're going to make him the leader of the free world all right the other guys what he's been in the he's been in the illinois state legislature for four years or some ridiculous thing hillary married well um on and on and on i mean you wouldn't i wouldn't put him in charge of three dairy queens in indiana and walk away you know but there they are and everybody's acting like they know something right i mean and believe me their consultants are taking money from them by the bucket loans by the bucket loans so you need a sense of superiority and you need to work on developing it and the best way to develop superiority is to recognize and acknowledge the idiocy ignorance and inferiority of everybody else around you i mean it's nice to be walking around thinking jeez i can't believe these people like first of all they're doing well in spite of themselves and it's a miracle they get to work in the morning incredible confidence donnie dutch's book um and donnie by the way built an ad agency for 300 mil which is a really good trick so it begs study in donnie dutch because you are selling nothing you got client well it's nothing what's an anagisive business is nothing i mean it's clients but they can leave and it's employees but they can leave there's nothing 300 million dollars remarkable um the title of his book uh it's one of those books you should you don't need to read it you just it's like books you should just prop up somewhere so you see the covers right you know i mean the title of his book is often wrong never in doubt okay and that's the kind of confidence you need because what people really want to buy from us is certainty they do not want to buy maybe's they want to buy certainty and they're happy to buy it if we're wrong the majority of the time as long as the times were right the economic payoffs okay but what they don't want to buy is maybe's money attitudes about money well we'll talk more about that tonight but the biggest attitude about money you need to have is steward wilds line when they show up bill them nothing for free nothing there's nothing more futile in life than dispensing free advice unless you insist on dispensing free advice to a family member if they don't give you money for everything for every step of the way you will lose control of them you will not get compliance and you will lose their respect their respect for you is in direct correlation to the fact that they must give you money for everything and don't make the mistake of oh man the guy's giving me a million dollars a year so i'll comp him to this big mistake big mistake don't comp him you might give him a break but don't comp him value real important to understand that in these businesses we are dealing with the client's value not our beliefs about value it's not about what we would buy it's not about what we would pay it's not about how we make decisions i was with a client the other day who publishes a news newsletter the newsletter for what it is in terms of content for someone who would use it in its industry is probably the best newsletter the most useful newsletter the most underpriced newsletter and the most valuable newsletter i've ever seen gives an enormous problem keeping subscribers because he's built the whole thing for people who actually use it of which in a niche of 30 000 there might be three i like it because i'm happy to zip through it and hurry there's no fluff there's no entertainment value there's no personality there's no nothing there's just information things to do great i'm thrilled give me that thing give me five minutes a highlighter let me find something to have somebody go fetch or somebody to go do and i'm on to the next thing there's him me and another three or four people so you got to sell the newsletter for like you know a hundred thousand dollars a year see that's not most people that's not their value system now they may enunciate it but it's not so this business is about not the core value of the service that has to be good they got to get results from whatever you give it but that's not what will keep them that's not what will make you money the the value is about the results to the client the client's perception of the results the relationship with the client so we're going to talk a lot about how you create value but the most important thing to know about value is it's all up to them it's not up to you and your attitudes about value have to switch to making them happy and having them want to continue in a relationship with you not about the core value see ron said a nifty thing you can go from 1100 to 30 000 i put the stuff in a better look at notebook well that didn't help the value at all you could deliver it in a paper sack it wouldn't make any difference but to the client in part because they actually can't judge the value of the stuff that's in the notebook so they don't have a basis for that so the notebook's pretty important guy gives you 30 000 bucks he expects a good look at notebook doesn't have a damn thing to do with the value but he expects a good look at notebook got to give him a good look at notebook last attitude has to do with the the willingness to exercise power and control over people and being comfortable with doing so because this is all about controlling them so the big speaker mistake the big speaker mistake in life is the attitude of i'm going to do such a good seminar and i'm going to give him so much information and i'm going to teach him so much stuff that i don't have to have a hard sell presentation at the front of the room and do a value build and do a takeaway and control that wholesale process to drive into the back of the room they'll they can recognize value they'll just go back and buy right there's like two speakers i've met in 30 years you get away with that it doesn't work logically it should but it doesn't work same thing in this business oh well if i if i put on a great meeting and i deliver great content and i'm a great coach and i really help them and their business improves and their income improves and their lifestyle improves and their health improves i shouldn't have to do all this manipulative stuff in order to keep control of them and keep them giving me money they'll recognize the value and it will take care of itself no it won't they will leave you in spite of all the core value that you deliver for all sorts of other reasons so you got to get comfortable with very deliberately and strategically exercising a lot of control over other people that's a very important thing there's an article i'm not going to take time with it now but there's a really good article i stuck in for you on some of the pages about superpowers but let's move past that and let's go to page five so here's of the big things we're going to talk about today here's here's big thing number one but what you will be paid for ain't what you think you're getting paid for and it's certainly not what your clients actually think they're paying for and it's certainly not what peers in the industry would think they're paying for what you're really getting paid for is your reputation far more so than your results your output the quality of your service you see the way watch your story is such a good example because this million dollar a year ceo who's running a multi-million dollar company owned by an even bigger conglomerate is making his entire decision on whether or not to run this ad campaign and spend another million dollars running it based on the reputation of benton barton durston and stupid he's not making it based on any objective standards he's not making it based on any discernment of his own he has none he has no ability to tell whether it's good advertising or bad advertising he's not making it based on any anything except benton barton durston and stupid made it so how did what did they really get paid for did they get paid for the advertising no they got paid for their reputation that's what they got paid for what is reputation well it's what they believe they know about you it's very significant that it that is not necessarily what you know about you and it is good for good or bad and it is not necessarily the truth good or bad you could be you could be the best financial planner on the planet but if the clients and prospective clients don't know that about you it is zero advantage you could be the worst financial but if they believe you're the best it's an enormous marketing advantage over the years working with chiropractors i've probably gotten adjustments from 300 of them the 10 best adjustments i ever got from the guys i believe are the 10 best chiropractors out of all of them are the poorest the best chiropractors don't have the best incomes why because they think their income has to do with being the best chiropractor no it has everything to do with being known for being the best chiropractor so it's about what do they believe about you so where does that come from well what do they surmise or assume about you so why in all the years have i never been challenged about educational qualifications for the most part because the assumption is made well you couldn't even be doing what you're doing without having them so why ask about them the vocabulary the language the way you carry yourself so part of what they believe they know about you is purely based on their assumptions that they make about you everybody who has a published book immediately gets assumptive credit for being smart and being an expert on the subject i know lots of authors including myself and neither of those things is necessarily true just because they got a book published with their name on it which they may or may not have written certainly doesn't guarantee any superior level of intelligence or any level of expertise on the topic it actually requires now for about 15 000 bucks you can get a really good mid-range ghost writer who in about two weeks can put together a perfectly respectable book on just about any topic that can be researched on which you can slap your name so all it really means is you were able to write a 15 000 check that's what it really means and it could mean nothing more than that years ago our friends at nsa they don't do it anymore they used to have an annual brochure contest not the stupidity of this to me was obvious and immediate that'd be relevant like if it was the national graphic artists association maybe or the national brochure makers association but the national speakers association is having a best brochure contest and so we took great trouble one year to make sure that lee won and so did you were you first or second or third what would you get first yeah so she got best brochure and she had the good sense and the lack of discretion to make the very comical statement that all it really proves is i give good brochure and that's all and that's all it really proves right but there's an assumption made ringer story and winning through intimidation is a useful story about all of that so how what else builds your reputation what they are told about you by others right so if you can engineer stories being told about you from person to person to person to person that creates reputation in fact it says you have to attempt to govern the stories that are told about you from person to person to person to person because those stories will control your reputation nobody will question the stories nobody will go investigate the stories you never need to worry about that the coral areas in hiring obviously one of the first things you should do when you hire anybody is check references everybody gets references you know what the national statistic is how many people hiring people ever check a reference four percent 96 never make a call they assume well if they listed them on the application as references they're only good to say good things about them so why bother calling them terrible assumption i've called and had them say all sorts of bad things about the person right what they read or hear about you real important so if you can get an article written about you the book in search of excellence he profiled some i don't know 30 companies in in search of excellence what's the biggest commonality about the excellent companies that were profiled in search search of excellence yeah bankrupt that's right they're all broke they're all in broke they may have been excellent for a while but then they were gone arguably they might have been putting too much emphasis on one thing excellence but well no i'm dead i'm deadly serious by the way well think about it there's something wrong there there's a cut there's another commonality there to be found in those companies that led to them all going broke that would be equally useful to know if not more useful to know than the commonality that they were all briefly excellent all right but my point is getting your company written about in that book for a period of time it drove your stock price up it caused people to want to come and do business with you do joint ventures with you buy you merge with you acquire you be acquired by you etc etc etc because they read about you in in search of excellence and in search of excellence books said you were an excellent company so you must be an excellent company why because that's what i read about you well first of all third of the companies were of course clients of the consulting company that peter's workforce so there's sort of a mixed agenda of deciding that they're the most excellent i mean but they read it it must be true and so what they read about you controls reputation now here's what's really useful to know about that there's not a great deal of distinction between what they read about you in real media and what they read about you in false media most don't make the distinction there was a thing on 2020 last night the pharmaceutical companies it's actually kind of comical the pharmaceutical companies are literally stealing all of the strategies and ideas that we've used in the illegitimate alternative health business for years that we go to jail for uh uh uh there the fdc doesn't bother them because they're pharmaceutical companies so they're stealing everything we always did and doing it and so they showed last night it was really pretty funny there's whatever pharmaceutical company it is they got a new prescription drug to keep you awake so because people fall asleep so they got this pill by the way all the research on it actually shows that it does less in shift workers versus the placebo it kept them awake 1.74 minutes longer a cup of coffee bought 3.6 minutes but we're going to put everybody on the drug right god bless that industry but here's the promotion piece that they've got for the drug they've got a fake american medical association journal of clinical psychiatry and it looks just like the real thing full of articles written by doctors that look just like real peer reviewed articles but of course all the doctors are on the payroll one way or another of the pharmaceutical company there is no peer review so the articles are really written by copywriters like me and the whole thing is being sent out to the doctors and they can't tell it apart from their regular journal of clinical satmar other and now we do this by the way we all go to jail in the alternative health business but in the real health business apparently now it's okay and it and it worked like gangbusters so all sorts of people its intent was to get them to prescribe the drug for off-label purposes right it's supposed to be being prescribed to like airline pilots and truck drivers and you know people who need to stay away now it's being prescribed as a antidepressant because this fake journal that went out to all these doctors has told them what they're not allowed to tell them on the label and the fake media is everybody's good as the good media very important for you to get it what they read about you is extremely powerful but but what they read about you in the wall street journal is only a little bit more powerful than what they read about you in the excellence street journal that looks like the wall street journal but exists only because we made it up what legends they hear repeated about you it's really important to like create tell and retell and tell a lot a handful of defining stories about yourself that are likely to be retold because when they are retold and retold and retold they take root better that they are true by the way or embellished from a place of truth but as a practical matter it wouldn't matter fifth who you are associated with and who they associate you with in their minds right now that's really important especially for someone who does not yet have a reputation in the market in which they are pursuing and wishes to create it and aura borrowed is nearly as good as an aura owned K Fed is actually able to earn money a remarkable tribute to one thing and one thing only being seen with and briefly married to Britney Spears and he is actually able to make money with no talent no ability no brains I mean it's remarkable isn't there's a there's a comedian first name is Tom I forget his last name up in years made a very good living for many many years and stored his money so he's very wealthy some of you would know him if I could think of his name thank you yeah what's his last name Dresen Tom Dresen and no disrespect but he's a journeyman comedian I mean his stand-up comedians go if we had a scale of one to ten we'd probably make him a five all right the Dresen's career was totally based on the Frank on the fact that Frank Sinatra let him open for him Stephen Edie Gourmet their career was built on Frank Sinatra I'm not sure they could have built a career just on Stephen Edie Gourmet but by borrowing an aura they were able to build a very sustainable career so who you are seen with who you are associated with who you co-author an article with who you interview for an article who you interview on a tape who you hang out with who you reference in your language and in your stories who you tell stories about his if you knew them all of that adds to your reputation long-time client of mine owns a business talk radio station actually owns two of them now and he has a show of his own which really is designed to promote his financial his investment management firm but one of the things that he was very good at from the beginning when all he had was a tiny little show on in San Antonio and he only had a few million dollars under management and he now has hundreds of millions of dollars under management is he was very good at conning people like Henry Kissinger and Alan Greenspan and John Travolta and the list is long to be on to be interviewed on his radio show which was a bought and paid for fake radio show on a dink-ass station in San Antonio and purely because they never ask and he had the Cajones to chase him he got Alan Greenspan for example who almost never gives interviews to anybody to be interviewed on his show 8 10 12 times and Henry Kissinger to be interviewed on the show and on and on and on and on and on and on and he intelligently mixed financial people of stature and credibility with celebrities of no stature or credibility other than that they are so celebrities and so he surrounded himself and associated himself with these people I'm always amused and I have some clients who I've gotten on a show I'm always amused when people then go on the show and now they like will list it in their promotional material that they were on so-and-so show right on such-and-such a station which means nothing nothing anybody because even though it's now a bigger show and it's a real show and it's in Houston unless you're in Houston nobody knows who the hell he is they don't make any difference but you could say a period on the Daniel Frishberg show with such people as Alan Greenspan Henry Kissinger John now the implication is you're all on the show at the same time together okay but what people want to draw from that is up to them just like in the speaking business we use the language shared the platform with and then as long as the people well that like implies we were all out there on a platform together no it might have been three days apart but they were on the same platform I was we were on the same event it doesn't know we did we don't know each other we didn't talk to each other we weren't in the same room at the same time but you get to use their names and you get to associate yourself with them so this business is about creating a reputation that you become known for the page first thing to do is decide what it's going to be all about decision who do you want to be you want to be the wise old grizzled veteran of your industry do you want to be you know the arrogant young punk who annoys everybody do you want to be obnoxious do you want to be warm and fuzzy do you want to be what do you want to be you get to decide because you're making it up from scratch and of course you ought to pick based on what's going to be effective for you now you can't totally be something you aren't so you got to pick characteristics you know that you can work with but effectiveness is what it's all about so there's decisions to be made about what you are going to set out to make yourself known as and known for you're known for competencies you're known as a type of person
